title: NZCI Flexi: Excavator Awareness
subtitle: Industry-Recognised Excavator Safety Training
description: Comprehensive excavator awareness and safe operation training aligned with New Zealand construction industry standards. Flexible online delivery for site supervisors, operators, and safety officers.

lessons.title: Excavator Components, Types and Pre-Start Checks
lessons.content:
## Excavator Basics — Components, Types and Pre-Start Checks

Excavators are one of the most versatile and widely used pieces of mobile plant in civil construction. Understanding how they work, their operating limits, and the critical importance of pre-start checks is fundamental to safe excavator operation.

## Main Components of an Excavator

| Component | Function |
|-----------|---------|
| **Undercarriage** | Tracks or wheels providing mobility and stability. Crawler (tracked) undercarriages for rough terrain; wheeled for road travel |
| **Slew ring (swing bearing)** | Allows the upper structure to rotate 360° on the undercarriage |
| **Upper structure (house)** | Contains engine, hydraulic system, counterweight, operator cab, and attachment mounting |
| **Counterweight** | Heavy steel casting at rear — balances the load on the boom |
| **Boom** | Primary arm structure attached to upper structure — typically fixed |
| **Dipper arm (stick)** | Second arm attached to boom tip — extends reach and digging depth |
| **Bucket/Attachment** | End attachment — bucket, hydraulic thumb, auger, compactor, grapple, hammer |
| **Hydraulic system** | Powers all movement — pump, control valves, hydraulic cylinders and motors |

## Types of Excavators

**By undercarriage:**
- **Crawler (tracked) excavators**: Most stable, best for rough terrain, soft ground, slopes. Cannot travel on sealed roads without low-loader transport
- **Wheeled excavators**: Road-legal, faster travel between sites, less stability than tracked. Common in urban civil work
- **Mini excavators (<10 tonnes)**: Compact, low ground pressure, suited to confined sites, trenching in residential areas

**By configuration:**
- **Standard (backhoe bucket)**: Digs below machine level — most common configuration
- **Long reach**: Extended boom and stick for deep or distant excavation (dredging, bank clearing)
- **Zero-swing / reduced tail-swing**: Upper structure does not protrude beyond track width — critical in confined urban sites

## Rated Operating Capacity and Stability

Every excavator has manufacturer-specified operating limits:
- **Maximum lift capacity**: Changes with boom/stick angle, slew position, and ground conditions
- **Lift charts**: Provided in operator manual — lifting over the side is typically less capacity than over the front
- **Never exceed rated capacity**: Exceeding limits risks tipping, boom failure, hydraulic failure
- **Slope limits**: Manufacturer's maximum working gradient (typically 20–35° depending on model)

**Stability factors:**
- Counterweight placement — never operate with counterweight bolts missing
- Ground bearing capacity — soft or saturated ground reduces effective stability
- Load position — extending boom fully reduces safe lift capacity significantly
- Swing position — side lifts less stable than front lifts

## Pre-Start Inspection

Pre-start inspections are mandatory before every shift. They are a legal requirement under HSWA (NZ) — the PCBU must ensure plant is fit for purpose before use.

**Operator pre-start checklist:**
- **Fluid levels**: Engine oil, coolant, hydraulic fluid, fuel, DEF (if Tier 4 engine)
- **Hydraulic hoses and connections**: Look for leaks, chafing, and damaged fittings
- **Track tension and condition**: Check for worn or damaged track pads, loose bolts
- **Undercarriage**: Rollers, idlers, sprockets — check for wear and damage
- **Bucket and teeth**: Secure attachment pins, worn or missing teeth, bucket cracks
- **Cab**: Controls function correctly, seat belt serviceable, ROPS intact, mirrors clean
- **Warning devices**: Reverse alarm functioning, lights operational
- **Walk-around**: Check for hazards (objects in work area, bystanders, services markings)

**Documentation:**
Pre-start inspections must be recorded — paper or digital form. Any defects found must be reported to the supervisor before the machine is operated. A defective excavator must be taken out of service until repaired.

## Operator Responsibilities

- Only operate equipment you are trained and authorised to use
- Complete pre-start inspection every shift
- Report defects immediately — do not operate defective plant
- Follow site-specific rules for exclusion zones and spotter requirements
- Never carry passengers — excavator cabs are single-operator
- Secure the machine when leaving (lowered attachment, engine off, doors locked)

## Key Takeaways

- Know the key components: undercarriage, slew ring, counterweight, boom, stick, bucket, hydraulics
- Choose the right excavator type: crawler for stability/rough terrain; wheeled for urban/road mobility
- Never exceed rated lift capacity — use lift charts and account for slew position
- Pre-start inspection is mandatory every shift — record and report all defects
- Defective plant must be taken out of service immediately

questions.q: What is the function of the counterweight on an excavator?
options: Provides additional hydraulic power for heavy lifts
Balances the load of the boom and attachment to prevent the machine tipping forward
Anchors the machine to the ground during trenching
Controls the swing speed of the upper structure
questions.explanation: The counterweight is a heavy steel casting at the rear of the upper structure that balances the weight of the boom, stick, and bucket/load. Without it, the machine would tip forward under load.

questions.q: A pre-start inspection reveals a hydraulic hose leak on the excavator. What must the operator do?
options: Continue work and report the leak at end of shift
Apply tape to the hose and continue light duties
Report the defect to the supervisor and take the machine out of service until repaired
Monitor the leak throughout the shift and stop if it gets worse
questions.explanation: Any defect found during pre-start must be reported immediately and the machine must be taken out of service until repaired. Operating defective plant is an HSWA breach — the PCBU must ensure plant is fit for purpose before use.

questions.q: Which excavator type is most appropriate for an urban confined site where the tail swing must not extend beyond the track width?
options: Long reach excavator
Standard crawler excavator
Zero-swing / reduced tail-swing excavator
Wheeled excavator
questions.explanation: Zero-swing (or reduced tail-swing) excavators are specifically designed so the upper structure does not protrude beyond the track width — critical for confined urban sites where structures, fences, or other hazards are close.

questions.q: An excavator's rated lift capacity at maximum boom reach over the side is 2 tonnes. What load is safe to lift in this position?
options: Up to 3 tonnes — the rating includes a safety factor
Up to 2 tonnes — must not exceed rated capacity for that position
Up to 4 tonnes if the ground is hard
Rated capacity only applies to lifting over the front
questions.explanation: Rated capacity for each position must never be exceeded. Over-the-side capacity is typically less than over-the-front. Exceeding rated capacity risks tipping, boom failure, or hydraulic failure regardless of ground conditions.

questions.q: Who must complete the pre-start inspection before an excavator is used each shift?
options: The site safety officer
The plant hire company
The operator — before each shift
The supervisor — weekly
questions.explanation: The operator must complete the pre-start inspection before every shift. It is the operator's responsibility to check the machine is fit for purpose before use — and to record findings and report defects.

lessons.title: Soil Types, Instability, Slope Work and Groundwater
lessons.content:
## Ground Conditions for Excavator Operations

Understanding ground conditions is critical for safe excavator operation. Ground conditions determine machine stability, excavation wall stability, bearing capacity for the machine, and the risk of sudden collapse.

## Soil Classification

New Zealand's varied geology produces diverse soil types on civil construction sites:

| Soil Type | Characteristics | Excavation Risk |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Rock** | High strength, predictable | Low collapse risk — may need blasting/hammering |
| **Gravel/cohesionless** | Free-draining, high friction angle | Stand-up time limited, water increases risk |
| **Sand** | Low cohesion, liquefaction risk | High — flows rapidly when disturbed or saturated |
| **Silt** | Low cohesion, highly erodible | Very high — unstable when wet |
| **Clay** | High cohesion when dry, loses strength when wet | Medium to high — swelling, softening, creep |
| **Peat/organic** | Very compressible, low bearing capacity | Very high — machine can sink |
| **Fill** | Variable — depends on fill material and compaction** | High — unknown history |

## Ground Bearing Capacity and Machine Sinking

The excavator applies significant ground pressure through its tracks:
- Soft, saturated, or peat soils may not support the machine's weight
- **Signs of inadequate bearing capacity**: Machine sinking, tracks not gripping, ground cracking around tracks
- **Mitigation**: Timber mats or Mexmat under tracks to spread load; reduce machine size; avoid saturated areas

**Track width matters**: Wider tracks = lower ground pressure per unit area. Mini excavators typically have lower ground pressure than large crawlers.

## Excavation Wall Stability

Excavation collapse is one of the leading causes of fatalities in civil construction. Wall stability depends on:
- **Soil type**: Cohesive soils (clay) stand longer; granular soils (sand, gravel) collapse rapidly
- **Depth**: Deeper excavations have greater lateral earth pressure
- **Water**: Groundwater and surface water dramatically reduce wall stability
- **Vibration**: Nearby plant, compactors, and traffic can trigger sudden collapse
- **Surcharge**: Excavated spoil, plant, and materials placed close to the edge increase load on walls

**Safe slope angles (general guidance — site-specific assessment required):**
- Firm rock: near vertical short term
- Stiff clay: 1:1 (45°)
- Cohesionless soil (sand/gravel): 1:1.5 (34°) or flatter
- Saturated soil: Much flatter or benching/shoring required

For excavations > 1.5 m depth in NZ: notifiable work — SSSP required, shoring or battering mandatory.

## Slope Work

Operating on slopes significantly increases rollover risk:
- Always check manufacturer's maximum working gradient
- Work up/down slopes — NOT across (side-sloping greatly increases rollover risk)
- Keep bucket low and close to machine when travelling on slopes
- Avoid swinging full loads across the slope
- Do not park on slopes without chocking tracks

**High risk slope scenarios:**
- Wet or frost-covered slopes — greatly reduced traction
- Working near slope edges — risk of edge collapse under machine weight
- Excavating cut faces on hillsides — risk of material above falling onto machine

## Groundwater and Dewatering

Groundwater presents multiple hazards:
- Reduces bearing capacity of surrounding soil
- Destabilises excavation walls
- Risk of water inrush — rapid flooding of excavation
- Risk of machine becoming stuck or sinking

**Dewatering options:**
- Sump pumping — remove water after it enters the excavation
- Wellpoint dewatering — lower groundwater table before excavating
- Sheet piling — exclude groundwater with interlocking steel sections

**Note**: Dewatered excavations can still collapse if the groundwater table rebounds — monitor continuously.

## Key Takeaways

- Know your soil type before excavating — it determines wall stability and machine support
- Excavations > 1.5 m: notifiable work in NZ — shoring or battering required
- Granular soils (sand/gravel) collapse rapidly without warning — never assume stability
- Slopes: work up/down, not sideways — keep bucket low when travelling
- Groundwater reduces stability dramatically — dewater and monitor continuously

questions.q: At what excavation depth does work become 'notifiable' in New Zealand, requiring an SSSP and shoring or battering?
questions.explanation: In New Zealand, excavations deeper than 1.5 m are notifiable work under HSWA regulations. A Site-Specific Safety Plan (SSSP) is required and the excavation walls must be shored or battered to a safe angle.

questions.q: Which soil type presents the highest risk of sudden collapse without warning in an excavation?
options: Stiff clay
Rock
Saturated sand or silt
Firm gravel above groundwater
questions.explanation: Saturated sand and silt have almost no cohesion and can liquefy and collapse instantly when disturbed. They offer no warning before collapse — workers in the excavation can be buried within seconds.

questions.q: An excavator needs to work on a 25° slope. What is the safest direction of travel?
options: Across the slope (side-sloping)
Diagonally across the slope
Up or down the slope (not sideways)
Direction does not affect rollover risk
questions.explanation: Always travel up or down slopes — never across (side-sloping). Side-sloping dramatically raises the centre of gravity and can cause rollover even within the manufacturer's maximum gradient rating.

questions.q: You notice the tracks of the excavator are sinking into the ground. What is the correct response?
options: Continue — sinking only matters if the machine tips
Stop immediately, exit safely, reassess ground bearing capacity, and consider timber mats or a different machine
Increase engine speed to power through
Move only the bucket to redistribute weight
questions.explanation: Sinking tracks indicate inadequate ground bearing capacity — a serious rollover and entrapment risk. Stop immediately, exit the machine safely, reassess the ground conditions, and use timber mats, Mexmat, or a lighter machine.

questions.q: Spoil excavated from a trench is stockpiled at the edge. Why is this dangerous?
options: It blocks the operator's view
It creates surcharge loading on the trench wall, increasing collapse risk
It prevents the machine from repositioning
It may contain underground services
questions.explanation: Spoil piled at the trench edge creates surcharge — additional vertical load on the wall. This increases lateral earth pressure and collapse risk. Spoil must be kept a minimum distance from the trench edge (typically >1 m or further for deep trenches).

lessons.title: 0800 DIG KNOW, Utility Types, Hand Dig Zones and Scanning
lessons.content:
## Underground Services — Awareness and Safe Excavation

Striking an underground service is one of the most serious hazards in civil construction. Consequences range from electrocution and explosion to major infrastructure disruption, significant financial liability, and prosecution under HSWA.

## 0800 DIG KNOW — The First Step

**Before ANY excavation, call 0800 DIG KNOW (0800 344 566)**

Beforeudig New Zealand (now integrated with Dial Before You Dig AU) provides:
- A centralised database of underground utility locations
- Plans and information from network operators (electricity, gas, water, telecom)
- Response typically within 2 business days
- Free service for contractors

**The 0800 DIG KNOW process:**
1. Contact at least 3 working days before excavation
2. Provide site address, excavation location description, depth estimate
3. Receive service location information from utility operators
4. Mark services on-site using colour-coded flags/paint
5. Brief all operators and workers on service locations

**Important**: 0800 DIG KNOW provides information only — it is not a physical locate. Always verify on-site.

## Types of Underground Utilities

| Utility | Hazard | Colour Code (NZUUG) |
|---------|--------|---------------------|
| **Electricity** | Electrocution, explosion, fire | Red |
| **Gas** | Explosion, asphyxiation, fire | Yellow |
| **Water** | High-pressure water burst, flooding | Blue |
| **Sewer/drainage** | Toxic gas (H2S), flooding, biological hazard | Brown/Orange |
| **Telecommunications (copper/fibre)** | Service disruption, financial liability | Orange/Black |
| **Traffic signals/streetlights** | Electrical hazard, service disruption | Purple |

**High-voltage electricity** is the most immediately life-threatening service. Contact with 11 kV or 33 kV distribution cables is typically fatal.

## Service Location — On-Site Verification

After receiving 0800 DIG KNOW information:
1. **Site survey**: Walk the alignment, identify surface markers (access covers, valve boxes, marker posts)
2. **As-built drawings**: Request from asset owner — note these may be inaccurate
3. **CAT and Genny scanning**: Cable Avoidance Tool (CAT) + Signal Generator (Genny) locate metallic services
4. **GPR (Ground Penetrating Radar)**: Locates both metallic and non-metallic services (plastic pipes, fibre)
5. **Potholing**: Expose service by hand excavation before machine excavation — the definitive method

**Accuracy of service location plans:**
Service plans are indicative only — typical accuracy is ±500 mm horizontally and ±300 mm vertically. Always treat services as potentially present and verify physical location.

## Hand Dig Zones

Once a service is located, a **Hand Dig Zone (HDZ)** must be established:

**Standard NZ hand dig zone requirements:**
- Minimum 300–500 mm each side of marked service location (check asset owner's requirements — may be 1.0 m for HV electricity)
- Hand tools only within the HDZ
- No machine excavation until the service is physically exposed and confirmed
- After physical exposure: machine can work outside confirmed service location with spotter guidance

**High-voltage electricity — special rules:**
- Contact the network operator before work within 1.0 m of HV cables
- Network operator may require de-energisation, cable relocation, or a network supervisor on site
- NEVER assume a power line or cable is de-energised until proven

## Overhead Services

Not just underground — overhead lines are also a major hazard:
- Maintain minimum 4.0 m horizontal clearance from overhead conductors with any part of the machine or load
- If clearance cannot be maintained, contact the network operator for line deactivation or rerouting
- Spotter required when working near overhead lines

## Key Takeaways

- ALWAYS call 0800 DIG KNOW before excavating — minimum 3 working days before
- Colour coding: Red=electricity, Yellow=gas, Blue=water, Orange=telecom, Brown=sewer
- Physical verification required: CAT scan, GPR, potholing — plans are indicative only (±500 mm)
- Hand dig zone: 300 mm–1.0 m each side of service — hand tools ONLY
- HV electricity: contact network operator before working within 1.0 m

questions.q: What is the first action to take before any excavation work in New Zealand?
options: Perform a visual inspection of the surface
Contact 0800 DIG KNOW at least 3 working days before excavation
Call WorkSafe NZ to register the excavation
Ask the site manager if services are present
questions.explanation: 0800 DIG KNOW (0800 344 566) must be contacted at least 3 working days before any excavation. This provides utility location information from network operators. It is the first mandatory step — even when you believe no services are present.

questions.q: What colour is used to mark underground gas services under the NZ utility colour code?
options: Red
Blue
Yellow
Orange
questions.explanation: Yellow marks gas services. Red = electricity, Blue = water, Orange/Black = telecom, Brown = sewer. Knowing these colours allows workers to immediately identify the utility type from surface markings.

questions.q: Service location plans from 0800 DIG KNOW show a gas main at 0.9 m depth on the alignment. How should the excavator operator proceed?
options: Excavate carefully with the bucket until the pipe is visible
Establish a hand dig zone 300–500 mm each side of the marked location and hand excavate to expose the service before machine work
Excavate to 0.5 m with the machine then stop
Skip the area and continue elsewhere
questions.explanation: A hand dig zone must be established around the marked service location. Hand excavation is required to physically expose and confirm the service location before any machine excavation nearby. Plans are accurate only to ±500 mm.

questions.q: What is the minimum horizontal clearance that must be maintained between an excavator and overhead electrical conductors?
questions.explanation: A minimum 4.0 m horizontal clearance must be maintained between any part of the machine (including the fully extended boom) and overhead electrical conductors. If this clearance cannot be maintained, contact the network operator for deactivation.

questions.q: You are potholing and expose an unmarked plastic pipe. What should you do?
options: Continue excavation carefully — plastic pipes carry water only
Treat it as a potentially live service, stop machine work, identify the utility owner, and inform the site supervisor
Mark it with spray paint and continue
Remove it from the excavation path
questions.explanation: Unmarked services exist — especially older or private infrastructure. Treat any unexpected underground pipe or cable as live until confirmed otherwise. Stop work, identify the asset owner through 0800 DIG KNOW or the client, and get confirmation before continuing.

lessons.title: Exclusion Zones, Spotters, Swing Radius and Overhead Lines
lessons.content:
## Safe Working Zones for Excavator Operations

The most common cause of excavator-related fatalities is workers being struck by the machine during slewing, reversing, or loading operations. Establishing and enforcing safe working zones is the most critical control for protecting ground workers.

## Exclusion Zones

An **exclusion zone** is an area around moving plant where no worker is permitted to be. For excavators, the exclusion zone must account for the full swing radius including the counterweight.

**Exclusion zone size:**
- Minimum = maximum reach of the machine (boom fully extended) + 1.0 m buffer
- Must also account for counterweight swing at rear
- Zone must be clearly marked with barriers, bunting, or physical boundaries

**Establishing exclusion zones:**
1. Before machine starts work, define and mark the exclusion zone
2. Communicate zone boundaries to ALL site workers at toolbox talk
3. No worker enters the exclusion zone while the machine is operating
4. Machine must stop slewing/moving before a worker can enter the zone with operator's permission
5. Where workers must enter the zone: use spotters and stop-work protocols

## Spotters

A **spotter** (also called a banksman or safety observer) is a dedicated worker who:
- Has full view of the working zone and potential hazard areas
- Communicates with the operator via agreed hand signals or radio
- Directs the machine clear of hazards (workers, services, structures)
- Has authority to stop the machine immediately

**Spotter requirements:**
- Must be fully focused — no other tasks while spotting
- Positioned where they can see both the machine and the hazard
- Must not be within the exclusion zone
- Agreed communication protocol established BEFORE work starts

**When is a spotter required?**
- When reversing with restricted operator visibility
- Working adjacent to workers or the public
- Near services, structures, or edge hazards
- Working in confined spaces

## Swing Radius Hazards

The counterweight swings in the opposite direction to the bucket. Workers focused on the bucket often do not see the counterweight swinging toward them.

**Critical facts about swing radius:**
- Counterweight extends 1.0–2.0 m behind the centre of the upper structure
- At full slew speed, the counterweight moves rapidly and is at head/chest height
- **Most struck-by fatalities involve the counterweight — not the bucket**
- Zero-swing machines reduce but do not eliminate this hazard

**Controls:**
- Exclusion zone must encompass the full counterweight swing radius
- Counterweight proximity detection systems available on modern excavators
- Worker induction must specifically address counterweight hazards

## Overhead Lines — Safe Approach Distances

| Voltage | Minimum Safe Distance |
|---------|----------------------|
| Low voltage (<1,000 V) | 4.0 m horizontal |
| High voltage 11 kV | 4.0 m horizontal |
| High voltage 33 kV | 4.0 m horizontal |
| High voltage 110 kV+ | Contact network operator |

**If work must occur within 4.0 m of any overhead line:**
1. Contact the network operator — Powerco, Vector, Transpower, or local lines company
2. Request de-energisation, insulation, or line relocation
3. Network may require their supervisor on site during work
4. Never assume a line is de-energised — always verify with network operator

## Traffic Management in Working Zones

When excavators work adjacent to live traffic:
- Traffic Management Plan (TMP) required
- Exclusion zone must account for machine swing AND vehicle movements
- STMS on site for high-speed roads
- Hi-vis on all workers at all times
- Spotters for reversing and positioning near traffic barriers

## Key Takeaways

- Exclusion zone = full swing radius (including counterweight) + 1.0 m minimum buffer
- Most struck-by fatalities involve the COUNTERWEIGHT — not the bucket
- Spotters: dedicated, full view, authority to stop machine — communication established before work
- 4.0 m minimum horizontal clearance from overhead lines — contact network operator if closer
- Never enter an exclusion zone while the machine is operating without operator permission and a stop-work protocol

questions.q: What is the most common cause of excavator-related struck-by fatalities on civil construction sites?
options: Being hit by the bucket during digging
Being struck by the counterweight during machine slewing
Falling materials from the bucket
Being hit by the tracks during machine travel
questions.explanation: Most excavator struck-by fatalities involve the counterweight — not the bucket. Workers focus on the bucket and don't see the counterweight swinging toward them at head/chest height. The exclusion zone must encompass the full counterweight swing radius.

questions.q: Who has the authority to stop the excavator immediately when acting as a spotter?
options: Only the site manager
The spotter — who has authority to stop the machine at any time
Only the operator
The safety officer from the office
questions.explanation: The spotter has the authority to stop the machine immediately — this is a core function of the spotter role. The operator must respond to the spotter's stop signal without question or delay.

questions.q: How large must an exclusion zone be for a standard excavator?
options: 2.0 m radius from the centre of the machine
The full counterweight swing radius only
The maximum reach of the boom fully extended plus 1.0 m buffer, encompassing the counterweight swing
5.0 m radius regardless of machine size
questions.explanation: The exclusion zone must account for the full reach of the boom when extended PLUS the counterweight swing radius at the rear, with a minimum 1.0 m buffer. It varies by machine size and configuration.

questions.q: Work is planned within 2.5 m of a 33 kV overhead power line. What must happen before work begins?
options: Post a spotter and continue
Contact the network operator to arrange de-energisation, insulation, or line relocation before work begins
Reduce machine speed when near the line
Excavate away from the line side only
questions.explanation: The 4.0 m minimum clearance cannot be maintained. The network operator must be contacted to arrange de-energisation, insulation, or line relocation BEFORE work begins. Never assume a line is de-energised until confirmed by the network operator.

lessons.title: NZQA Unit Standards, Medical Requirements and Operator Responsibilities
lessons.content:
## NZ Excavator Operator Certification

Operating heavy plant in New Zealand requires demonstrated competence. NZQA unit standards provide the national qualification framework for excavator operators, ensuring operators have the knowledge and practical skills to operate safely.

## NZQA Unit Standards for Excavator Operation

Key unit standards for excavator operators through MITO (Motor Industry Training Organisation) and other ITPs:

| Unit Standard | Title |
|--------------|-------|
| US 17285 | Operate a tracked excavator |
| US 17286 | Operate a wheeled excavator |
| US 17283 | Carry out pre-operational checks on mobile plant |
| US 3799 | Demonstrate knowledge of safe digging near underground services |
| US 17277 | Demonstrate knowledge of ground conditions for plant operation |

**Assessment structure:**
- Written knowledge assessment (theory)
- Practical assessment on actual equipment
- Assessor must be registered and approved by MITO or equivalent ITO
- Achievement recorded on NZQA National Student Number (NSN)

## NZ Certificate in Civil Infrastructure Works

The NZ Certificate in Civil Infrastructure Works (Level 3 and Level 4) incorporates plant operation:
- Level 3: Foundation skills in civil construction — includes plant operation units
- Level 4: Advanced practice — supervisory and specialist skills

The NZCI (NZ Certificate in Infrastructure) Flexi-option allows workers to select relevant units matching their role — ideal for specialised plant operators.

## Operator Authorisation

Holding a unit standard does NOT automatically authorise operation on a specific site. The PCBU must:
- Verify the operator's competency and qualifications
- Provide site-specific induction and orientation
- Formally authorise the operator to use specific plant on that site
- Keep records of authorisation

**Graduated authorisation:** Operators may be qualified but restricted to specific tasks, conditions, or machine types (e.g. authorised for mini excavator only, not large track machine).

## Medical Requirements

Excavator operators must be medically fit to operate plant safely:
- Vision: Adequate visual acuity — depth perception is critical for precise excavation work
- Hearing: Adequate hearing to respond to verbal instructions and audible alarms
- Medical conditions: Epilepsy, diabetes, cardiac conditions, or medications affecting consciousness/reaction time must be declared
- Fatigue: Operators must not operate plant when fatigued — PCBU fatigue management policy applies

**Fitness for duty:**
Operators must not operate plant under the influence of alcohol, drugs, or medications that impair judgment or reaction time. Site drug and alcohol policies apply.

## Ongoing Operator Responsibilities

Certification is the start — ongoing responsibilities include:
1. **Daily pre-start**: Complete and record pre-start inspection every shift
2. **Report defects**: Immediately report any faults or damage
3. **Update skills**: Attend refresher training when new attachments, machines, or methods are introduced
4. **Site rules compliance**: Follow site-specific rules, exclusion zones, and safe work procedures
5. **Near miss reporting**: Report all near misses — these are early warning signs
6. **Stay current**: Check for updated unit standards and best practices periodically

## Consequences of Unauthorised Operation

Operating plant without authorisation:
- Is a breach of HSWA — worker can be personally prosecuted
- Voids insurance for any damage or injury caused
- May result in immediate dismissal
- Creates serious risk of injury or death to the operator and others

## Key Takeaways

- Unit standards via MITO/ITOs — US 17285 (tracked), US 17286 (wheeled) are core qualifications
- Holding a unit standard does not mean you are authorised on every site — PCBU must verify and authorise
- Medical fitness: vision, hearing, no impairing conditions — fitness for duty every shift
- Pre-start, defect reporting, near miss reporting: ongoing daily responsibilities
- Unauthorised operation breaches HSWA — personal prosecution risk, insurance void

questions.q: What NZQA unit standard covers operating a tracked excavator?
questions.explanation: US 17285 covers operating a tracked excavator. US 17286 is for wheeled excavators, US 17283 is for pre-operational checks, and US 3799 is for safe digging near underground services.

questions.q: An operator has a NZQA unit standard for tracked excavator operation. Can they immediately start operating on a new site without further process?
options: Yes — the unit standard is sufficient authorisation nationwide
No — the PCBU must verify competency, provide site induction, and formally authorise operation on that specific site
Yes — as long as the operator informs the supervisor
Only if the operator has more than 2 years experience
questions.explanation: A unit standard demonstrates competency but does not automatically authorise operation on every site. The PCBU must verify qualifications, conduct site-specific induction, and formally authorise the operator to use specific plant on that site.

questions.q: An excavator operator has been prescribed medication for a health condition that causes occasional drowsiness. What should they do?
options: Operate normally — it rarely affects them
Disclose the condition to their employer and seek medical clearance before operating plant
Operate only during mornings before the medication takes effect
Self-assess each day and decide whether to operate
questions.explanation: Any medical condition or medication that could affect consciousness, reaction time, or judgment must be disclosed to the employer. The operator must obtain medical clearance before operating plant. Fitness for duty applies every shift.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of the NZCI (NZ Certificate in Infrastructure) Flexi-option?
options: A qualification only for supervisors
Allows workers to select relevant unit standards matching their specific role — flexible specialisation
An overseas recognition pathway only
A refresher qualification for experienced operators only
questions.explanation: The NZCI Flexi-option allows workers to select relevant units that match their specific role — enabling specialisation (e.g. plant operator, traffic management, pipe laying) without completing the full certificate. It is ideal for workers building targeted skills.
